title: The better AI gets, the smaller its share of the economy might get – Alex Imas and Phil Trammell
summary: Dwarkesh Patel interviews economists Alex Imas and Phil Trammell on how AGI economics will reshape capital vs. labor shares, tax policy, wealth redistribution, and global inequality. They discuss whether demand collapse is likely, integration of humans into machine economies, and strategies for developing countries to benefit from AI gains.
writeup: An interview with Alex Imas (Director of AGI Economics at Google DeepMind and Professor at University of Chicago) and Phil Trammell (Head of Economics at Epoch AI and Stanford research scholar) on what economic theory predicts for wages, labor share, and scarcity under AGI-level automation. The central tension: the Kaldor fact that labor has claimed roughly 60% of GDP for centuries despite prior waves of automation, and whether AI represents a qualitative break from that pattern. The through-line is that individual forecasts are unreliable, good data on consumer demand elasticities and job flows is nearly absent, and the most rigorous path is mapping scenarios to the specific conditions of scarcity that would generate them.
key_points: The 'relational sector' is defined as goods and services where the consumer pays a premium specifically because a human is in the loop: a barista, a doctor delivering a diagnosis, a performer on stage. This is the leading candidate for persistent human economic value even in a fully automated world, but reliable conjoint-analysis data on willingness to pay for human versus machine tasks does not yet exist.

David Ricardo's Industrial Revolution predictions are a warning about economic forecasting: his specific job-displacement predictions proved accurate, yet prime-age employment in 2026 is at its second-highest recorded level ever, because structural change made automated goods cheap and freed spending for new services. Ricardo missed this because his model fell into what is now called the lump-of-labor fallacy, the assumption that new jobs would not be created.

Labor's share of GDP has held near 60% through the Industrial Revolution and every automation wave since, a regularity economists call a Kaldor fact. Atkinson's accounting-consistent analysis finds labor share has not measurably fallen over the long run, though the last 20 to 30 years are contested depending on measurement conventions.

Network-adjusted capital share, which accounts for all labor embodied down the full supply chain rather than only the final production step, shows that even highly automated goods like US computer and electronic products carry roughly 50% labor share. Nothing has yet been fully automated end-to-end. The coming qualitative shift is the first goods whose entire supply chain can be automated, driving network-adjusted capital share to one with no step requiring human involvement.

The task-based model of jobs argues that most roles are bundles of tasks: a doctor spends most time on insurance forms and pharmaceutical calls, not on patient diagnosis. Automating all but the relational task may still classify the job as 'relational sector' if consumers pay a premium for the human touch on that final component, making ordinary service workers, not performing artists, the correct reference class for this analysis.

Alex Imas calls for a 'Manhattan Project for data': economists currently lack consumer demand elasticities, real-time tracking of which jobs are being created or destroyed, and a reliable version of the O*NET task database, all of which are necessary to distinguish between the full-employment relational-sector scenario and a collapsing-labor-share scenario.

chapters.title: Introduction: Economics of Automation and AGI
David Ricardo and the Lump-of-Labor Fallacy
Data Gaps and the Manhattan Project for Data
Kaldor Fact: Labor Share Stability Over Centuries
Network-Adjusted Factor Shares and Full Automation
Task-Based Jobs and the Relational Sector in Practice
